intent: Check whether the candidate understands JWT as a signed token format and does not confuse encoding, signing, and encryption.
primaryQuestion: What is a JWT, what are its three parts, and is the payload encrypted by default?

requirements.description: Header carries token type and signing algorithm or key ID metadata.
Payload carries claims.
Signature protects integrity using the signing key.

expectedSignals.label: Explains Base64URL encoding is not encryption.

requirements.description: JWT parts are typically Base64URL encoded and can be decoded by anyone who has the token.
Payload should not contain secrets or sensitive personal data unless encrypted separately.
Signature validates that the token was issued by a trusted signer and was not altered.
Signature does not hide payload contents.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions JWS versus JWE at a high level.

requirements.description: Signed JWTs are common for authentication tokens.
Encrypted JWT or JWE is a different mechanism when confidentiality is required.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands short-lived access authorization and longer-lived renewal with reduced blast radius.
primaryQuestion: Explain the access token plus refresh token flow. Why are two separate tokens used?

requirements.description: Access token is presented to protected APIs to authenticate the request.
It is usually short-lived to limit damage if stolen.
Refresh token is used to obtain new access tokens without asking the user to log in again.
It is stored and protected more carefully than access tokens.
API rejects expired access token or client notices expiry.
Client sends refresh token to renewal endpoint.
Server validates refresh token and issues new token pair or access token.

expectedSignals.label: Explains why one long-lived access token is weaker.

requirements.description: Long-lived bearer access token gives attackers longer API access if stolen.
Separate refresh flow allows rotation, reuse detection, and revocation.

intent: Check whether the candidate can reason about token lifetimes as risk management rather than fixed magic numbers.
primaryQuestion: What expiration strategy would you use for access tokens versus refresh tokens, and what happens when an access token expires?

expectedSignals.label: States access tokens should usually be short-lived.

requirements.description: Explains lifetime depends on risk, UX, and system needs.
Avoids claiming one universal expiration value is always correct.

expectedSignals.label: States refresh tokens are longer-lived but more tightly controlled.

requirements.description: Refresh tokens require secure storage, rotation, and revocation strategy.
Refresh token lifetime may be absolute and/or sliding.
Server checks exp and rejects expired access tokens.
Client should not rely only on local decoding as security enforcement.
Valid refresh can issue new token pair.
Invalid, expired, revoked, or reused refresh token should require re-authentication or incident handling.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands JWT verification without session lookup and the revocation/control trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: JWT is often called stateless. What does that mean, and what is the trade-off compared with server-side sessions stored in Redis?

requirements.description: API can verify signature and claims without loading session state for every request.
Token carries claims needed for basic authentication decision.
Less central session storage lookup can simplify horizontal scaling.
Services can validate tokens if they have trusted keys and claim rules.
A valid JWT remains usable until expiry unless additional revocation checks are added.
Changing user status or permissions may not immediately affect already issued tokens.
Redis sessions are easier to revoke centrally.
They add storage dependency, lookup latency, and operational state.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands how refresh token rotation limits replay attacks and detects stolen refresh tokens.
primaryQuestion: What is refresh token rotation, token family, and refresh token reuse detection?

requirements.description: Each successful refresh returns a new refresh token.
The previous refresh token is invalidated after use.

expectedSignals.label: Defines token family at a high level.

requirements.description: A token family is the chain of refresh tokens descended from one login session.
The server tracks current valid token or family state.
If an old refresh token is used again, it may indicate theft or replay.
Server should revoke the token family or require reauthentication depending on policy.
Rotation requires server-side state for refresh tokens, hashes, family IDs, or revocation records.
Purely stateless refresh rotation is not enough for reuse detection.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can compare token storage choices under XSS, CSRF, UX, and implementation constraints.
primaryQuestion: Where should JWTs be stored on the client: localStorage, sessionStorage, or HttpOnly cookies? What are the security implications?

expectedSignals.label: Explains localStorage and sessionStorage XSS exposure.

requirements.description: JavaScript can read tokens stored in web storage.
An XSS bug can steal bearer tokens from storage.

expectedSignals.label: Explains HttpOnly cookie benefit and CSRF consideration.

requirements.description: HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie.
Cookies are sent automatically, so CSRF protections may be needed depending on SameSite and API design.
HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, Path, and Max-Age or Expires are considered.
Secure requires HTTPS for transmission.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids claiming one storage is universally perfect.

requirements.description: Choice depends on threat model, app architecture, domain setup, mobile/browser needs, and CSRF/XSS mitigations.
Refresh token and access token may use different storage strategies.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands revocation strategies and device/session state for JWT-based systems.
primaryQuestion: How do you invalidate or revoke a JWT before it expires, and how would you implement logout for one device versus all devices?

expectedSignals.label: Explains short-lived access tokens as baseline damage control.

requirements.description: Short expiry limits how long stolen access tokens work.
Short lifetime does not immediately revoke an already issued token.
Stores revoked jti or token identifiers until expiry.
API checks denylist for protected requests when immediate revocation is required.
Logout one device revokes that device refresh token or token family.
Logout all devices revokes all refresh token families for the user or increments a session version.
Blacklist checks add storage lookup and operational complexity.
Immediate revocation requires some server-side state.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands token claims as security-critical assertions that require validation.
primaryQuestion: What are common JWT claims such as iss, sub, aud, exp, nbf, iat, and jti, and why must the server validate claims and signature instead of only decoding?

expectedSignals.label: Explains registered claims at a useful level.

requirements.description: iss identifies issuer, sub identifies subject, aud identifies intended audience.
exp, nbf, and iat describe validity timing.
jti is a token identifier useful for replay tracking or revocation.
Signature proves token integrity and trusted issuer when using the correct key.
Decoding alone does not prove the token is valid.
Issuer prevents accepting tokens from untrusted issuers.
Audience prevents using a token meant for another API or client.

expectedSignals.label: Explains time validation and clock skew.

requirements.description: exp and nbf must be checked server-side.
Small allowed clock skew may be used, but excessive skew weakens validity windows.

Authentication claims help identify the principal.

Critical authorization may require database or policy lookup.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids putting too much authorization data in tokens.

requirements.description: Large tokens hurt performance and leak internal permission structure.
Keeps claims minimal and purposeful.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands symmetric versus asymmetric signing and basic JWT algorithm safety.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between HS256 and RS256, what is kid used for, and why restrict allowed algorithms?

requirements.description: HS256 uses a shared secret for signing and verifying.
RS256 uses a private key for signing and public key for verification.

expectedSignals.label: Explains when asymmetric signing is useful.

requirements.description: Multiple services can verify tokens with public keys without knowing the signing private key.
Key distribution and blast radius can be improved.

expectedSignals.label: Explains kid for key selection during rotation.

requirements.description: kid identifies which key should be used to verify the token.
Server must still only trust keys from its configured trusted key set.
Server should restrict accepted algorithms instead of blindly trusting alg from the token header.
Accepting none or unexpected algorithm can create critical vulnerabilities.

Missing, malformed, expired, or invalid tokens lead to authentication failure.
API should avoid logging full tokens.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands account state changes as revocation and authorization events, not only login events.
primaryQuestion: How should tokens be handled when a user changes password or an admin disables the account?

expectedSignals.label: Revokes refresh tokens on sensitive account changes.

requirements.description: Password change should invalidate existing refresh tokens or sessions depending on policy.
Admin disable should prevent renewal and future authenticated access.

expectedSignals.label: Handles access tokens with short lifetime or immediate deny checks.

requirements.description: Short-lived access tokens reduce residual access.
Immediate enforcement may require checking user status, token version, or denylist.
Password change may log out all devices or all except current device based on product policy.
Admin disable should usually affect all sessions.
Security-sensitive events should be logged.
User may receive notification for password change or suspicious revocation.

intent: Check whether the candidate can evaluate immediate revocation against latency, availability, and operational state.
primaryQuestion: What are the trade-offs of checking a token blacklist on every request?

requirements.description: Blacklist or denylist blocks known revoked tokens before expiry.
It is useful for logout, compromise response, or admin revocation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains lookup cost and availability risk.

requirements.description: Every protected request may need a fast shared lookup.
If Redis or blacklist store is unavailable, the system must choose fail-open or fail-closed behavior.
Entries should expire when the token would naturally expire.
Storing token identifiers or hashes is safer than storing raw tokens.
Short access-token lifetime may be enough for lower-risk cases.
Refresh-token revocation plus short access tokens can reduce per-request checks.

intent: Check whether the candidate maps authentication responsibilities to NestJS request lifecycle correctly.
primaryQuestion: In NestJS, where would JWT verification usually live: middleware, guard, interceptor, or service? Why?

expectedSignals.label: Chooses guards for route-level authentication and authorization checks.

requirements.description: Guard can decide whether the request may proceed to the route handler.
Guard has access to ExecutionContext and route metadata.
Middleware can parse tokens or attach preliminary request data.
Middleware is less suited for route metadata based authorization decisions.
Interceptor is better for wrapping execution, logging, caching, or transforming responses.
Service can contain token verification helper logic used by the guard but should not replace lifecycle placement.
After verification, request user or context can be populated for downstream handlers.
Authorization should still check permissions and resource ownership.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands time-based token validation and why small leeway may be needed without weakening token lifetime too much.
primaryQuestion: What is clock skew, and how can it affect exp and nbf validation in JWTs?

requirements.description: Clock skew is a time difference between systems such as issuer, verifier, and client.
Distributed systems cannot always assume clocks are perfectly aligned.
A verifier clock that is ahead may reject a token slightly before the issuer intended.
A verifier clock that is behind may accept a token slightly longer than intended.
A verifier clock that is ahead may accept a not-before token too early.
A verifier clock that is behind may reject a token that should already be valid.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions bounded leeway and clock synchronization.

requirements.description: Small configured leeway can reduce false rejections.
Leeway should be limited, and systems should still use clock synchronization such as NTP.

Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.

ja.title: Clock skew and time-based JWT claims
ja.displayQuestion: 次の点を説明してください: What is clock skew, and how can it affect exp and nbf validation in JWTs?
ja.displayIntent: この質問は clock_skew を実務で説明できるかを確認します。
guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。
